response:
You’re definitely past “hobby” territory, but you’re still in the very early, lumpy stage. Learning about LLCs now makes sense; actually doing an S‑corp election can probably wait until your profit is more consistent.

Here’s how this shakes out:

1. What your data shows

Over the last 12 months:

Self-employment-style income

$0 from June 2025–April 2026
$13,960 of Business Income in May 2026 (into Bluevine Business Checking 8810)

Business-type expenses

$0 June 2025–March 2026
$4,200 in April 2026
$7,052.30 in May 2026
Categories: Business Rent, Software, Business Travel, Office Supplies, Health Insurance, etc.
Across business-linked accounts (Bluevine, Ally Tax Reserve, Capital One 360, Chase Ink, Amex Blue Business), April–May 2026 expenses total $9,702.72

Rough net profit

Income: $13,960
Expenses: $9,702.72
Approx net profit: $4,200–$6,900 depending on which expenses you treat as business-related

So you’ve got a dedicated business checking account, business credit cards, and clearly business-type expenses. That’s already “real business” behavior, even if it’s just getting started.

2. When people typically explore an LLC

An LLC is mostly about:

Legal separation (liability protection)
Cleaner separation of business vs personal finances
Professional appearance and easier admin as you grow

Common patterns that often justify considering an LLC:

Pattern | Why it points toward an LLC
---|---
Regular business income (even if modest) | Shows it’s an ongoing business, not a one-off gig
Business-specific expenses and tools | Indicates you’re operating as a business (software, rent, travel)
Contracts with clients or vendors | Liability and professionalism become more important
Risk exposure (services, physical products, employees) | More reason to separate personal assets from business risks
Multiple business accounts/credit cards | You’re already treating it like a business operationally

How you compare:

You already have:

Business checking and business credit cards
Business-labeled expenses (rent, software, travel, office supplies, health insurance)

And:

A meaningful month of revenue ($13,960) and nearly $10k of expenses in a short window

So even though the income is new and not yet consistent, your behavior looks like someone running a real business. That’s usually when people start exploring:

Whether an LLC would help with liability protection
Whether it would make bookkeeping and taxes cleaner

This is more of a legal/structure question than a “do I make enough profit yet?” question.

3. When people typically explore an S‑corp election

An S‑corp election is usually about tax optimization, not basic structure. It tends to matter when:

Net profit is consistently above a certain level

Many tax pros start to see potential benefit when annual net profit is roughly:
$40k–$60k+ per year and expected to continue or grow
Reason: S‑corps can reduce self-employment tax by splitting income into:
“Reasonable salary” (subject to payroll taxes)
“Distributions” (not subject to self-employment tax)

The business is ongoing, not one-off

S‑corp adds complexity: payroll, separate tax filings, stricter bookkeeping
That extra work usually only makes sense if the business is stable enough to justify the cost and admin

You’re already treating it like a business

Separate accounts, regular invoicing, recurring clients, etc.

How you compare:

Profit level

You have roughly $4,200–$6,900 of net profit over a very short period (April–May 2026).
If that’s a one-time spike, it’s usually too early to worry about S‑corp mechanics.
If this is the start of a pattern that will average, say, $4k–$6k net profit per month going forward, that’s a very different story and would put you in the range where many people at least model S‑corp scenarios.

Stability

Almost no activity for 10 months, then a sudden burst of income and expenses.
That looks like a business that’s just launching or just landed a big project, not yet a stable, predictable operation.

So right now you’re more in the zone of:

“Early-stage business that’s worth understanding LLC basics now”
“S‑corp is something to model later if this level of profit becomes consistent”
4. Practical next steps to consider

Not tax or legal advice, but these are common moves for people in your spot:

Clarify your trajectory

Ask: if the last 1–2 months are typical going forward, what would 12 months of:
Gross revenue
Expenses
Net profit
look like?
If you’re on track for $40k–$60k+ of annual net profit, that’s when S‑corp conversations often start to become meaningful.

Keep your business finances clean
